description: To render spoken, signed, or recorded language in writing or another stable textual form. A transcription may be verbatim or edited, complete or partial, certain or uncertain according to its stated method. 'thekasi' (caption) travels in time with an event; 'sitheku' can stand afterward as a document.
articulatory_notes: Send unvoiced air through a narrow alveolar groove for 's' and raise the tongue into high-front unrounded 'i'. Move the tongue tip to the upper teeth for unvoiced 'th'; keep the air flowing and release into stressed true-mid front unrounded 'e'. Seal the back of the tongue at the soft palate for unvoiced 'k', then finish in high-back rounded 'u'.
sound_symbolism: The fine hiss moves from the alveolar ridge to dental breath, then ends in a firm velar closure. That gradual gathering of airflow can call to mind passing speech caught in a stable line.
usage_notes: The object is the speech, signing, recording, or other language rendered as text. Noun use gives a transcription or the act of making one. Source and method matter, as do editing, completeness, and uncertainty. A transcription records expression; agreement, interpretation, and authorship remain separate.

pillars.peace-linguistics-practices: The verb separates making a record of expression from agreeing with it, interpreting it, or speaking for its author.
pillars.pre-industrial-wisdom: Writing lets a spoken line remain after the breath has gone. It also carries what the scribe heard, missed, or chose, which is why the method belongs beside the record.

semantic_domains.communication: carrying spoken or signed expression into writing
semantic_domains.creation: making a stable textual rendering of language
semantic_domains.temporal: preserving passing language for later use
